title: Global Prosperity Gap has halved in the last four decades
subtitle: While the global Prosperity Gap has decreased over the last four decades, regional and national differences are substantial
source: Authors’ calculation using Poverty and Inequality Platform (version March 2026). https://data360.worldbank.org/en/int/indicator/WB_WDI_SI_SPR_PGAP.
visdescription: This chart reports the trend in Prosperity Gap from 1981 to 2026. It reports the trends for the world, regions, and select countries.
keyFactLabel: behind the rest of the world, Sub-Saharan Africa continues to face a significant gap in prosperity.

scenes.text: The Global Prosperity Gap has more than halved from over [emphasis: 13 ]in [emphasis: 1981 ]to  less than [emphasis: five ]in [emphasis: 2026]. This substantial progress reflects the poorer nations and people catching up to the richer nations and people.
All regions have made significant progress since 1981, with East Asia and Pacific standing out, having the largest (21) to the third lowest gap (2.5).

But the most recent decade has seen stagnation in the Prosperity Gaps in two regions: Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan & Pakistan and Sub-Saharan Africa, with the latter’s Prosperity Gap today close to the global gap in the 1980s. By this metric, the region is around 40 years behind global progress.
Colombia’s and Peru’s Prosperity Gaps have experienced similar developments in recent decades, including periods of increasing gaps, most notably during the COVID-19 pandemic.
In Ethiopia, the level today is half what it was in the 1980s, although this hides faster progress in the 1990s and early 2000s, and a stagnation in recent decades.
Worryingly, in some Sub-Saharan African economies, such as Mozambique, improvements in the Prosperity Gap have stalled over the last two decades, and recently worsened.
